⚡ SPARK Centre About Apr 2026 Jun 2026 Jul 2026 The Data Reflection
SPARK Centre · Mornington Peninsula · April to July 2026

Making Thinking Visible at the SPARK Centre

Three sessions, two thinking routines, and a collaboration with Karin Morrison and Project Zero at Harvard Graduate School of Education, documented in full, from Karin's Year 2 masterclass to our own Year 6 sessions.

Kieran Nolan, Educational Technologist & Katie Gunn, SPARK Teacher
The Setting

About the SPARK Centre

The SPARK Centre is a hands-on technology and innovation classroom located within a small rural primary school on Victoria's Mornington Peninsula, serving a largely coastal, semi-rural community.

The centre exists on a simple premise: that primary students are capable of far more sophisticated technical and intellectual work than they are usually offered. Students in the SPARK Centre work on proper gaming computers with keyboards, not tablets. They have built and published their own arcade games, run their own networks, competed in cybersecurity capture-the-flag challenges, 3D-printed their own designs, and learned public-private key cryptography years before most curricula would introduce it. On the drawing board are plans for students to design prosthetic arms and build VR environments for people living with dementia, framing technical skills around real community problems.

The program is led by Kieran Nolan, Educational Technologist, and Katie Gunn, SPARK Teacher, who co-teach across year levels. The classroom culture of the SPARK Centre is built around respect, empathy and determination, and encourages reflection, goal-setting and respectful dialogue: exactly the conditions that thinking routines depend on.

Adding Visible Thinking Routines to the SPARK program reflects a deliberate philosophy: that genuine understanding, not just technical skill, is the goal. We want students who can build things and think things. Across the three sessions documented in this report, the technology captured the data; the routines did the thinking work.

The three sessions

April 2026 · Year 2

"What Do You Think You Know About AI?" A Morning of Thinking Out Loud

Facilitated by Karin Morrison · Documented by Kieran Nolan, Educational Technologist

Seven-year-olds, it turns out, have a lot to say about Artificial Intelligence. Some of it is surprisingly accurate. Some of it is wonderfully imaginative. And all of it is exactly the kind of thinking that great teachers live for.

In April 2026, the Year 2 class gathered in the SPARK Centre for a visit from Karin Morrison (educator, researcher, co-author of the landmark book Making Thinking Visible, and the in-school leader of the pilot of Project Zero's Cultures of Thinking project) for a session that turned the room into a live, colour-coded map of young minds at work.

The routine: Think, Puzzle, Explore

The thinking routine Karin brought to the SPARK Centre was Think, Puzzle, Explore, one of the cornerstone routines from Project Zero at Harvard Graduate School of Education and a highlight of Making Thinking Visible (Ritchhart, Church & Morrison, 2011). Simple in structure but rich in purpose, the routine is designed to activate prior knowledge, surface genuine questions, and spark curiosity that propels further investigation.

Think, Puzzle, Explore: How It Works
A three-part sequence from Project Zero's Thinking Routines Toolbox that makes existing knowledge and curiosity visible before new learning begins.
01
THINK
What do you think you know about this topic? Share existing ideas and prior knowledge, right or wrong, certain or hazy.
02
PUZZLE
What questions or puzzles do you have? What are you genuinely curious or confused about?
03
EXPLORE
How might you explore further? What strategies, resources, or next steps could help you investigate?

The beauty of Think, Puzzle, Explore is that it meets students exactly where they are. There are no wrong answers in the "Think" phase, offering honest snapshots of what learners bring into the room. For a topic as enormous and fast-moving as AI, this is a particularly important starting point.

What the Year 2s think they know about AI

Armed with the SPARK Centre's gaming computers, keyboards at their fingertips, and Karin's encouragement, the Year 2 class added their ideas to a shared digital space for the very first time, using Padlet as a collaborative, real-time sticky-note wall. The "Think" section of the board filled up quickly, and the range of responses was a delight.

What Year 2 think they know about AI: a selection from the Padlet
it means Artificial Intelligence
AI is funny
AI can help you find stuff!
AI is smart.
the agents in Minecraft are AI
AI can do fund stuff and turn you into an animal
ai is smart.n/nj.
THE AGENT
MINCRAFT

The responses reveal something important: these students already have a mental model of AI, even if they can't fully articulate it yet. The recurring references to Minecraft's agents show that AI isn't abstract to this generation; it's something they've already encountered through play. And the delightfully cryptic contributions are a perfectly honest reflection of what it feels like to be at the very edge of your understanding and still willing to jump in.

"Why can't AI be smarter?" (Year 2 student, SPARK Centre)

The questions they're puzzling over

If the "Think" section showed us what students bring in, the "Puzzle" section showed us where their minds want to go next. The questions the Year 2s posted were thoughtful, funny, and in some cases genuinely philosophical.

What questions do you have about AI?
What is AI?
Why can't AI be smarter?
Is AI smart?
Can you use photos for AI?
Is YouTube AI?
YouTube is not AI?

"Is YouTube AI?" generated some spirited discussion, a question that even many adults would struggle to answer precisely. The productive tension between "Is YouTube AI?" and "YouTube is not AI?" appearing on the same board captures exactly what this routine is designed to surface: genuine, unresolved thinking that is ready to be investigated.

First time on Padlet, and they loved it

For every student in the room, this was their very first time using Padlet, and the collaborative digital notice board turned out to be a perfect match for the energy of the session. Students could see each other's ideas appearing as they typed, sparking conversations and a healthy amount of "ooh, that's a good one."

Karin then turned the same thinking routine back on the tool itself. A second Padlet board asked the class: What do you think you know about Padlet? and What questions do you have about Padlet? The responses were quick and precise; these students had just learned by doing, and they knew it.

What Year 2 now knows about Padlet
put ideas on it
write on it
erase and draw on it
Could the whole school use Padlet at the same time?
Can I use Padlet at home?
What else can I do with Padlet?

"Could the whole school use Padlet at the same time?" That question, posted by one student, landed perfectly. It showed not just understanding of the tool, but imaginative thinking about its possibilities. That's exactly the kind of transfer that Karin's work is designed to foster.

About the book: Making Thinking Visible: How to Promote Engagement, Understanding, and Independence for All Learners by Ron Ritchhart, Mark Church, and Karin Morrison (Jossey-Bass, 2011).

Thinking Routines Toolbox: The full library of Project Zero's thinking routines is freely available at pz.harvard.edu/thinking-routines.

Wednesday 24 June 2026 · Year 6

Year 6 Students Step Into Others' Shoes on the Social Media Ban

Circle of Viewpoints · Facilitated by Kieran Nolan, Educational Technologist & Katie Gunn, SPARK Teacher · 35 responses

What does a parent actually feel about Australia's new social media law? Or a content creator whose young audience has just been legislated away? Or a mental health professional who has watched teenagers scroll themselves into anxiety for years? These were not hypothetical questions at the SPARK Centre on Wednesday 24 June 2026.

They were the starting points for a rich thinking session that saw Year 6 students wrestle with one of the most contested policy decisions in recent Australian history, and do it from someone else's point of view.

A routine with real depth

The session was a Circle of Viewpoints (COV), a structured thinking routine developed by Project Zero at Harvard Graduate School of Education. Unlike a traditional debate, COV does not ask students to argue a side. Instead, it asks them to inhabit a perspective: to think, question, and reason as if they were a parent, a politician, a teacher, a social media company, a mental health professional, or a content creator. It works especially well for complex, controversial topics where students may initially only see two sides.

The topic was Australia's law banning social media access for young people under 16, a Commonwealth law that took effect in December 2025, which gave students plenty to think about. Students were guided to respond through the routine's sentence starters:

The Circle of Viewpoints sentence starters
01
I am thinking of the topic from the viewpoint of [chosen viewpoint]…
02
I think… (describe the topic from your viewpoint: be an actor and take on the character)
03
A question I have from this viewpoint is…
04
After considering this viewpoint, has your thinking changed?

"The beauty of this routine is that it disrupts the instinct to immediately form an opinion. Students who might have come in with a fixed view found themselves having to genuinely defend a position they had never considered before."

Kieran Nolan, Educational Technologist, SPARK Centre

The data tells its own story

35
Total responses
6
Viewpoints explored
51.4%
Chose the Parent viewpoint
ViewpointResponsesPercentage
Parent1851.4%
Content creator / influencer1028.6%
Mental health professional411.4%
Politician12.9%
Teacher12.9%
Social media company12.9%

More than half of the students chose the Parent viewpoint, with Content Creator / Influencer the next largest group. The distribution itself became a talking point: why did so many students gravitate toward the Parent perspective? Perhaps because parents are the most visible stakeholder in this debate, the ones making daily decisions about screen time at home. The real-time pie chart, filling live on screen as students submitted, became a discussion point in itself: a map of where the class stood before any discussion had even begun.

What students actually said

The responses were striking in their range and, in many cases, their sophistication. Students adopting the Parent viewpoint were broadly supportive of the ban, though not uniformly so, citing screen addiction, inappropriate content, and the loss of outdoor activity, while raising practical enforcement concerns: what happens when a determined teenager simply uses a parent's face ID? Content Creator responses pushed back firmly on economic grounds, but several went further than simple opposition and proposed alternatives. Mental Health Professional respondents were largely in favour: "It is good that it has finally happened," one wrote, "because kids were just scrolling for hours."

"What if you made it more protective instead of making it 16+, so everyone can have fun too? Even lowering the age would be better than 16+. Maybe try to focus more on what people want as well as what they need. Include safety updates, but include both 50/50."

Year 6 student, Content Creator viewpoint

The single student who adopted the Politician viewpoint produced perhaps the most considered response of the session, declining to simply agree or disagree and instead proposing a targeted alternative: restrict commenting on short-form content across platforms rather than blocking access entirely. They also identified the ban's central weakness as their key question: what if kids find ways to get around the age verification?

The full responses, by viewpoint

Every documented response from the session, exactly as collated in the session report. Tap a viewpoint to expand.

Parent viewpoint: 18 students (51.4%)
Thinks about banMain benefits / concernsQuestion raisedThinking changed?
I think it is good so my kids aren't on a screen all day.It will let me spend more time with them.Why they did this I don't know, but I do know I will get a lot more time with my kids.I think it is good because it will let me spend time with my family and they will go outside more.
I'm so glad my child isn't addicted to their screen. Now my child can have a professional job and earn money and have their own life outside of a screen.This will teach my children the ways of the real world and realising how things are really done. Maybe I can even get them to do their homework.Is this ban really full proof or can people find ways out of it?No, it really hasn't, because I originally thought it was a great idea, we need to have a life outside of our screens.
That you are safe and that kids don't see things online that could be inappropriate or untrue.Stuff online can be bad for kids to see.Should kids have access to social media?No, because the internet isn't always safe for kids.
Annoying because my kid keeps asking to get into my account.My kids are safer on the internet.What is the point of the social media ban?Still unsure.
Good so kids don't get addicted, like my daughter who was on her phone all the time instead of reading books.So kids don't get addicted and waste all their time on devices.We should consider banning social media, it's bad for kids' eyes.My thinking will never change because it has always been bad for kids.
I think it's good for kids because sometimes online they can see things they are not meant to see. When they are older they can manage it better.This will make children have a better childhood and grow up not always on their devices. My concern is them just asking parents to do face ID or claiming they are over 16.It should be more child-proof, as lots of kids might make an account when they are too young.At first I thought it wasn't the best idea, but after writing this I believe it's a good idea.
I think it's a great way of getting kids off social media so they won't experience fake content.Kids can get off social media and spend more time outside or with family.What if kids under 16 got onto social media by lying about their age?It hasn't changed because I already knew why social media was banned and why parents care.
As a parent, I believe the social media ban is a great opportunity for kids to become more helpful around the house, spend time with family and friends, and be more active.The ban has benefitted my whole family, we spend more time together watching a movie, playing a board game, or doing chores.What if we made a phone ban at school throughout the day, and kids could have their devices back after school?My thinking has not changed. I still believe the ban age might be slightly too high, perhaps 14 or 15 instead of 16.
It is bad and kids should get to keep going on social media.Kids should get to keep the permission to go online.Why do they have to quit?No change.
It helps get kids off the internet and stops them going onto things they shouldn't. Blue light from screens also ruins young children's eyes.The concern is that kids these days aren't experiencing full days of life and burning energy as they should.Should kids experience more of the outside world?No, my thinking has stayed the same.
Being a parent is hard enough and social media being taken away means children can't learn some life skills. On the other hand, it is a benefit because they can be creative, spend time outside, and avoid inappropriate content.My main concern is kids seeing inappropriate content or content that might make them feel uncomfortable or unsafe.What if social media started doing age-appropriate programs for different age groups?It has stayed the same.
It glues kids to their phone, makes their eyes suffer, and is destroying our future.Kids will not be smart enough to apply for a job because they're glued to their phone.With this law, it is making kids go outside and be creative.Yes.
Kids' eyes can suffer from too much screen time. If they are not on a screen they are outside.Kids get smarter.What are the consequences for underage access?I've always thought that.
Kids under 16 are still able to live life, and it's not too late to keep it that way. Social media can also teach life lessons so I think it's either/or.Probably social media bullying, and you can talk to anyone in the world. But it is a fun way to talk to friends.What will be done if a kid creates an underage account, what are the punishments?I just don't know if it's bad or good.
It is good for my kids to talk to other people and learn their own lessons in the real world.There is less bullying on social media for people 16 and older.How would you make sure kids under age don't access it any way?No.
I think social media shouldn't be banned because if there's something you need to look up on YouTube and it's banned, you don't know what to do.If social media wasn't banned, you can talk to people online and see what people on Facebook Marketplace have.No specific question raised.No, I have no change to my answer.
I think having social media is like not making you do anything, like going to play outside.The benefits are that you're not getting stressed and you see your friends.No specific question raised.No, I think my answer is not changing.
Content Creator / Influencer viewpoint: 10 students (28.6%)
Thinks about banMain benefits / concernsQuestion raisedThinking changed?
Bad, because there is nothing wrong with it.Content creators make money from videos. If there's no young audience, no one will earn money.Why did you ban it in the first place?No, because what's the point of banning it.
This is so horrible! Now kids won't see my videos.I will lose followers, subscribers, and views.What if kids under 16 need to learn something and they can't use videos because YouTube is blocked?It has not. Why do kids get banned for what other people post? Why not make a kids' version with a clean version of the platform?
Absolutely terrible for me because now I have a lot fewer viewers and subscribers.Concern, my channel might not make enough money now because all my videos were directed to kids.Why did they decide to do that? I am very cross because I'm going to be broke.No.
Horrible that I can't do my favourite thing and show people new content I find.People can forget about me, my viewers or online friends.What if you made it more protective instead of making it 16+, so everyone can have fun? Even lowering the age would be better than 16+. Maybe try to focus more on what people want as well as what they need, include safety updates, but include both 50/50.A little bit, since I thought about it.
I think it's bad because now content creators can't reach as wide an audience and earn money.Concerns: not getting enough views or money. What if YouTube, TikTok and other platforms go out of business because of this ban? Should content creators give up?Why did they do this to kids under 16? In my opinion it should be kids under 12.No, because I dislike the ban. It has made kids upset because now they have nothing to watch on road trips, long flights, or when they're bored.
I think it was a little bit random and I have a lot of concerns, in particular, companies like Apple abusing face recognition for many apps.Roblox has ads showing a parent and child playing together, but the child in the ad looks about 6 and the allowed age for Roblox is 10+.What if someone used an older sibling for face recognition to bypass the age check?A little bit, since I thought about it more.
Mental Health Professional viewpoint: 4 students (11.4%)
Thinks about banMain benefits / concernsQuestion raisedThinking changed?
Good for young kids and early teens.It is good that it has finally happened because kids were just scrolling for hours.Why had this not been a law earlier?No, because I already have no interest in social media myself.
Good, I can play games outside with my brothers.Benefits: they play games with friends outside now.I like this new ban because I can go on bike rides.Yes, because I can do other things with my friends.
Politician viewpoint: 1 student (2.9%)
Thinks about banMain benefits / concernsQuestion raisedThinking changed?
I don't agree but don't disagree. I think they should just stop being able to comment on videos, Shorts, TikTok, Instagram, and Snapchat.Predators, inappropriate content, and short-form content that drives endless doom-scrolling.What if kids find ways to get around the age verification?No.
Teacher viewpoint: 1 student (2.9%)
Thinks about banMain benefits / concernsQuestion raisedThinking changed?
It's good.No specific benefits noted.How would it help me?Unclear.

Key themes and observations

In favour of the ban: screen time reduction (more outdoor activity, family time and focus, especially from the Parent viewpoint); child safety (inappropriate content, online predators, misinformation); mental health benefits (less anxiety, doom-scrolling and dopamine-driven compulsive behaviour); and a potential reduction in cyberbullying.

Against the ban: the economic impact on creators who lose a young audience; the educational value of platforms like YouTube that a full ban may restrict; and enforcement concerns around age verification, platform compliance, and whether determined young people could simply bypass restrictions.

Nuanced positions: several students suggested alternatives to a blanket ban, including age-restricted but moderated "kids" versions of platforms, comment restrictions rather than full bans, or a lower age threshold such as 12 or 14. The Politician viewpoint student showed sophisticated understanding, acknowledging both sides while proposing a targeted feature restriction rather than a full ban.

Did their thinking change?

Perhaps the most telling data point from the session is what happened at the end. Students were asked whether their thinking had changed after inhabiting their chosen viewpoint. Many said no, but "no" rarely meant they were unchanged. A notable number of students reported that their thinking shifted, or deepened, during the exercise, even when they ultimately maintained their original position: they could articulate it more precisely, with more awareness of the opposing view, than at the start. This is consistent with the intent of the routine: not to change minds, but to develop more considered, multi-perspective understanding of complex issues.

Not changed minds. Bigger ones.

Monday 20 July 2026 · Year 6

Think, Puzzle, Explore: Artificial Intelligence, on a Live Wall

Facilitated by Kieran Nolan, Educational Technologist & Katie Gunn, SPARK Teacher · Classes 6A and 6B · 39 responses

On Monday 20 July 2026, Year 6 students took part in a Think, Puzzle, Explore routine on Artificial Intelligence in the SPARK Centre: the same routine Karin Morrison had modelled with the Year 2s, now run with the school's oldest students and the SPARK Centre's own technology stack.

The session began with the students' own thinking rather than with instruction, giving us a clear map of the class's current understanding, curiosities and misconceptions.

How the session was delivered

Responses were collected through an anonymous Google Form containing three paragraph questions (Think, Puzzle, Explore), a class selector (6A or 6B) and an optional 1 to 5 self-rating of how well students feel they currently understand AI. No names or email addresses were collected.

A custom-built live response wall (a single-file web application created for the session) pulled responses from the published spreadsheet and displayed them in three colour-coded columns on the SPARK Centre's new interactive touchscreen. As students submitted their thinking, their responses appeared on the wall in near real time, anonymously, tagged only by class.

This combination proved powerful. Anonymity lowered the barrier to contribution, so students who might not raise a hand in discussion still put their thinking on the wall. At the same time, seeing responses appear live on the big screen created energy and momentum in the room, and gave the whole group a shared object to think with. The touchscreen allowed facilitators and students to scroll through and revisit responses together during the closing discussion.

Participation and self-rated understanding

A total of 39 responses were received across the two Year 6 classes: 23 from 6A (59%) and 16 from 6B (41%). All 39 respondents also rated how much they feel they understand AI right now, on a scale of 1 (not at all) to 5 (very well).

RatingStudentsShareInterpretation
1410.3%Little or no confidence
237.7%Low confidence
3923.1%Moderate confidence
41538.5%Confident
5820.5%Very confident

The mean self-rating was 3.5 out of 5, and 59% of students rated themselves 4 or 5. Year 6 students clearly see themselves as reasonably knowledgeable about AI, which is unsurprising given how visible AI tools have become in their daily lives. However, the written responses tell a more nuanced story: while students readily named AI products and everyday uses, their puzzles reveal significant gaps around how AI actually works, where it came from, and what its limits are. This gap between confidence and depth of understanding is itself a valuable finding, and exactly the kind of insight the TPE routine is designed to surface.

What students said

Representative responses from both classes, drawn from the live wall. Minor spelling corrections for readability; original meaning preserved.

Think: What do you think you know about AI?
Student response (verbatim)Class
When I hear someone say Artificial Intelligence, I think of what AI can do to help and what it doesn't do to help. I think that AI is used more for schoolwork, planning or maybe learning how to fix something.6A
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a lot of codes put together to make a superhuman robot, that can answer anything you ask it. Although AI is extremely intelligent, it still hasn't mastered the arts of acting like a human. You can still tell by little hints if something is made by AI.6A
AI is not a living thing but it has a brain of a human. It can get you answers or it can write your work.6B
I think it is pretty interesting because who knows what else it does, and it's pretty useful too, but it's still horrible for people.6B
A smart resource to figure out problems and other stuff.6B
There are apps and you can use it for advertisement and videos. Such as robots and ChatGPT, Gemini.6B
I think Megamind lives inside a computer and makes AI work.6B
Puzzle: What questions or puzzles do you have?
Student response (verbatim)Class
Who created AI?6A
What is AI not better than humans at?6A
I wonder how many people use AI on a daily basis? How does AI think of their knowledge so quick?6A
How does AI think of so many questions and create so many things at once so quickly? Who made AI?6A
How does AI know this stuff?6B
How is it so smart?6B
Who made AI and why?6B
How long did it take to code and make AI?6B
What would happen if we didn't have AI?6B
Why do people use AI photos and videos?6B
Explore: How could you find out more?
Student response (verbatim)Class
How to make AI have self consciousness.6A
I could ask AI itself, or I could ask my SPARK teachers who know a lot about coding and how things work.6A
You can use ChatGPT and Gemini and many more.6A
You can use Google.6A
Because you can find answers.6B
I can use AI.6B
To videos and the news and things that happen with AI.6B
What if they put a Microsoft chip AI in someone's brain, what would happen?6B

Five key themes

1. AI as a helper for everyday tasks. In the Think column, students most commonly described AI in terms of what it can do for people: help with schoolwork, planning, answering questions and fixing things. Several also noted its limits, such as the observation that AI "still hasn't mastered the arts of acting like a human." This shows an emerging critical stance: students are not simply dazzled by AI, they are already noticing its tells and shortcomings. One 6B student captured the ambivalence many feel, describing AI as "pretty useful too, but still horrible for people."

2. Origins, or who made this? The single most common puzzle across both classes was about where AI came from. "Who created AI?" and "who made AI?" appeared repeatedly. Students encounter AI as a finished product and have little visibility into its history or the people behind it. This is fertile ground for future exploration, opening onto the history of computing, Alan Turing, machine learning research, and the companies building today's systems.

3. How does it work, and how is it so fast? A second cluster of puzzles focused on mechanism and speed: how AI "thinks of their knowledge so quick," and how it can "create so many things at once so quickly." Students perceive AI's speed and scale as almost magical, which signals a need to demystify training data, pattern recognition and computation. One student's playful theory that "Megamind lives inside a computer and makes AI work" is a reminder that, absent a real mental model, students will fill the gap with whatever explanation is at hand.

4. Limits and consciousness. Some of the most sophisticated thinking appeared at the edges: one student asked what AI is not better than humans at, and another wanted to explore how to make AI have self-consciousness. These questions touch on genuine open problems in the field and show students independently arriving at questions that researchers and ethicists are actively debating.

5. Students know where to look. In the Explore column, students named a healthy range of pathways: asking AI tools directly (ChatGPT, Gemini), searching Google, and, notably, asking their SPARK teachers "who know a lot about coding and how things work." Students see both digital tools and trusted humans as legitimate sources, a strong foundation for teaching source evaluation and critical use of AI.

The closing discussion

The session ended with a whole-group discussion built directly from the Puzzle column on the touchscreen. We scrolled through the wall with the students, drawing out the recurring questions and inviting the group to suggest ways each could be investigated. On the origins of AI, the group discussed tracing the question historically: when the term was first used, who the early pioneers were, and how today's AI companies fit into that story. On how AI works so quickly, students suggested asking AI tools to explain themselves and testing those explanations against what their SPARK teachers and reliable sources say. On AI's limits, the group talked about designing their own experiments: finding tasks where humans still outperform AI, and trying to catch the "little hints" that reveal AI-generated work. And on consciousness, the discussion acknowledged this as an open question that even experts disagree on, modelling for students that some puzzles do not have settled answers, and that this makes them more worth exploring, not less.

The discussion transformed the Puzzle column from a list of questions into a genuine inquiry agenda, with students leaving the session owning both the questions and the plans for exploring them.

Reflections and next steps

The anonymous live wall on the new touchscreen materially changed participation: every student's thinking was visible and valued without anyone being put on the spot, and the real-time display kept engagement high throughout. The gap between high self-rated understanding (mean 3.5/5) and the foundational nature of the puzzles raised is one of the routine's clearest findings. The Puzzle column now stands as a student-generated map of the cohort's curiosity, with origins, mechanism, limits and consciousness as its four anchor themes. Re-running the same self-rating after future thinking routine sessions will allow a direct before-and-after comparison of student confidence, with the TPE responses providing qualitative evidence of growth.

Across the Sessions

What the Data Tells Us

3
Sessions across two year levels
74
Structured Year 6 responses captured digitally
3.5/5
Year 6 mean self-rated AI understanding
SessionCohortRoutine & topicCaptureFacilitatorWhat it showed
April 2026Year 2Think, Puzzle, Explore: AIPadlet (first use), open shared wallKarin MorrisonPlay-anchored mental models (Minecraft agents); genuine unresolved questions; the routine turned on the tool itself
24 June 2026Year 6Circle of Viewpoints: social media banGoogle Forms, 35 responses, live pie chartKieran & KatieHeavy skew to the Parent viewpoint (51.4%); sentence starters produced longer, reasoned argument; thinking sharpened more often than it changed
20 July 2026Year 6Think, Puzzle, Explore: AIGoogle Forms + custom live wall, 39 responses (6A: 23, 6B: 16)Kieran & KatieConfidence (3.5/5 self-rating) alongside visible misconceptions; a student-authored map of the cohort's curiosity

Three findings stand out when we put the sessions side by side.

The questions persist; the vocabulary matures. A Year 2 asked "Why can't AI be smarter?" in April. A Year 6 asked "What is AI not better than humans at?" in July. These are the same question, four year levels apart, and neither cohort has had it answered. Both year levels also anchored AI in their own media worlds: Minecraft agents for the 2s, ChatGPT, Gemini and Google for the 6s. Prior knowledge about AI, at every age we tested, arrives through the technology students already play with. That is where our teaching should start.

Where students stand is data before anyone speaks. The COV viewpoint distribution was itself a finding. Students gravitated to the stakeholders most visible in their daily lives (the people who set screen-time rules at home and the people they watch online) and largely avoided the institutional perspectives. The live pie chart made that skew visible to the class in real time, and asking why the room had distributed itself that way became one of the richest discussion moments of the session.

Confidence and understanding are different measurements. The Year 6s rated their own AI understanding at a mean of 3.5 out of 5, comfortably above the midpoint. The wall behind that number told a more complicated story: definitions built on "superhuman robots," uncertainty about who makes AI, and Explore strategies that ranged from thoughtful (ask the AI itself, ask our SPARK teachers) to circular (Google it). The gap between the self-rating and the responses is not a criticism of the students. It is exactly the diagnostic picture Think, Puzzle, Explore exists to produce, and it is far more useful to us than the number alone.

Teacher Reflection

What We Learned Watching, Then Doing

By Kieran Nolan, Educational Technologist & Katie Gunn, SPARK Teacher

There is a particular kind of learning that only happens when you watch someone who wrote the book do the thing the book is about, and then have to do it yourself. From April to July this year, that has been our professional development in miniature: first observing Karin Morrison run Think, Puzzle, Explore with our Year 2 students, then stepping up to facilitate two thinking routines of our own with the Year 6 cohort.

Watching a master at work

When Karin ran Think, Puzzle, Explore with the Year 2s, we expected to learn something about the routine. What we actually learned was mostly about restraint. Karin never rushed a response. Fragments were welcomed with the same seriousness as full sentences. When a student typed a string of letters that meant nothing to anyone but themselves, it stayed on the board, because the "Think" phase has no wrong answers, and a seven-year-old willing to jump in at the very edge of their understanding is doing exactly what the routine asks. Watching her hold that space, without correcting, hurrying, or quietly editing the board, was the single most instructive thing either of us saw all year.

Watching versus doing

Karin's session looked effortless, and running our own showed us where the effort actually lives: in the discipline of not filling silence, not steering responses, and trusting the routine to do the work. With the Year 6s we caught ourselves, more than once, wanting to prompt a "better" answer. The routines only produce honest data if the facilitator can tolerate honest answers, including lazy ones. Alongside genuinely sophisticated thinking, the July wall carried blunt sentiment and the throwaway responses that anonymity invites. We chose to see all of it as data. A wall that only showed polished answers would tell us far less about where the cohort actually is.

Age changes the form of thinking, not its presence

The Year 2 board was fragments, exclamations and glorious near-nonsense; the Year 6 responses were paragraphs and positions. But the underlying moves (activating what you know, naming what puzzles you, and imagining how to find out) were recognisably the same. If anything, the Year 2s were more fearless. Anonymity gave the Year 6s a different kind of courage, and occasionally a licence for silliness that the younger students, posting to a shared visible wall with Karin beside them, never sought.

The two routines ask different things of students

Think, Puzzle, Explore surfaces the thinking a student already has. Circle of Viewpoints asks them to build thinking they don't yet have, from inside someone else's shoes. Running both with the same cohort a few weeks apart made the contrast vivid: the TPE data maps what the class knows; the COV data shows what the class can do with a perspective. Together they are a far more complete picture of the cohort than either alone. The most encouraging COV finding was the end-of-session check: most students said their thinking hadn't changed, but their "no" usually meant they could now defend their position with more precision and more awareness of the opposing view. Not changed minds. Bigger ones.

The technology should be the quietest thing in the room

We went from Karin's Padlet to Google Forms to a custom-built live wall on the SPARK touchscreen, and the lesson at every step was the same: the tool's job is capture and visibility, nothing more. Padlet gave the Year 2s the thrill of seeing everyone's thinking appear at once. Forms gave the Year 6s anonymity and gave us clean, analysable data. The live wall gave the room a shared object to think with. In every case, the routine did the thinking work; the technology just made it visible, which is, after all, the whole point.

Acknowledgements

Our deepest thanks go to Karin Morrison, co-author of Making Thinking Visible and the in-school leader of the pilot of Project Zero's Cultures of Thinking project. Karin initiated the collaboration with Project Zero that made this entire arc of work possible, and her generosity in the SPARK Centre (modelling the routines, mentoring us as facilitators, and connecting our students' thinking to a global research community) is the foundation everything in this report is built on.

Thanks also to Professor Ron Ritchhart and Project Zero at Harvard Graduate School of Education, whose Visible Thinking research gave us the routines themselves, and to the Year 2 and Year 6 students, whose honest thinking made these sessions what they were.
